training.title: Caffeine — 3–6mg/kg, 30–60 min before
training.detail: Most studied ergogenic aid in sport. Increases strength output by 3–7% and delays perceived effort. Black coffee counts.

training.title: Creatine — 3–5g pre or post workout
training.detail: The single best-evidenced supplement for strength and muscle. Take it consistently every day; timing around training gives a slight edge.

training.title: Whey protein — 30–40g within 45 min post
training.detail: Fast-digesting whey spikes amino acid availability during the critical post-workout window. Leucine content is what drives muscle protein synthesis.

training.title: Casein protein — 30g before bed
training.detail: Whey handles post-workout; casein handles overnight. Together they keep muscle protein synthesis elevated for 18+ hours after training.

training.title: Omega-3 — 2–3g EPA/DHA with food
training.detail: Amplifies muscle protein synthesis when combined with strength training. Take with your largest meal for best absorption.

rest.title: Creatine — 3–5g with food
rest.detail: Take daily regardless of training. Creatine stores deplete if you skip rest-day doses. Timing doesn't matter on rest days — just stay consistent.

rest.title: Omega-3 — 2–3g EPA/DHA
rest.detail: Anti-inflammatory effect is strongest on recovery days. Reduces exercise-induced muscle damage and accelerates readiness for the next session.

rest.title: Casein protein — 30–40g before bed
rest.detail: Slow-digesting protein feeds muscles overnight for 5–7 hours. The longest fasting window of your day — don't waste it with nothing.

rest.title: Magnesium — 300–400mg at night
rest.detail: Supports deep sleep and muscle relaxation. Most athletes are sub-optimal. Glycinate and malate forms absorb significantly better than oxide.

match.title: Caffeine — 3–6mg/kg, 60 min pre-match
match.detail: Peak blood concentration at 60 min. Standard 200mg dose is enough for most. Avoid if you don't use it in training — race day is not the time to experiment.

match.title: Electrolytes — pre and during
match.detail: Sodium, potassium, magnesium. Sip a sports drink in the warm-up and at half time. Hyponatremia (over-drinking plain water) is a real match-day risk.

match.title: Creatine — skip on match day if new
match.detail: If you're already taking creatine daily, continue as normal. If not, don't start on match day — GI discomfort risk during play is not worth it.

match.title: Whey protein — 40g within 90 min post-match
match.detail: Matches cause as much muscle damage as a hard gym session. The post-match protein window is as important as post-gym. Don't skip it.

match.title: Tart cherry juice — 30ml, 2x daily
match.detail: High in anthocyanins which reduce post-match DOMS by 20–30%. Best started 3–4 days before a match and continued for 2 days after.

sport.title: Caffeine — 3mg/kg, 45 min before practice
sport.detail: Lower dose than gym days — practice requires sustained output, not short maximal efforts. Reduces perceived effort and keeps reaction time sharp.

sport.title: Electrolytes during practice
sport.detail: Sweat losses during sport practice are often underestimated. Sodium-containing drinks outperform plain water for sessions over 60 min.

sport.title: Whey protein — 30–40g within 45 min post
sport.detail: Sport practice causes significant muscle protein breakdown. A post-session shake is the fastest way to flip the balance toward synthesis.

sport.title: Creatine — 3–5g with food
sport.detail: Daily maintenance dose. Keep it consistent every day of the week — storage levels are what matter, not per-session timing.
